note_data.title: Group 17 (Halogens)
keyPoints: **Halogens** are Group 17 elements with seven electrons in their outer shell and form halide ions with charge -1
**Electronegativity** decreases down Group 17: F > Cl > Br > I, affecting bond polarity and reactivity
**Atomic radius** increases down the group due to additional electron shells, weakening attraction to nucleus
**Oxidising ability** decreases down Group 17: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂ because larger atoms gain electrons less readily
**Displacement reactions** occur when a more reactive halogen displaces a less reactive halogen from its halide solution
**Disproportionation** occurs when chlorine reacts with cold dilute alkali forming chloride and chlorate(I) ions
**Hydrogen halides** show increasing thermal stability down the group: HF > HCl > HBr > HI
**Silver halide precipitates** have different colours: AgCl (white), AgBr (cream), AgI (yellow) for halide identification
**Reducing ability** of halides increases down the group: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ > F⁻ due to decreasing ionisation energy

definitions.definition: A reaction where a more reactive halogen replaces a less reactive halogen from its halide compound
A redox reaction where the same element is simultaneously oxidised and reduced
The ability of an atom to attract electrons in a covalent bond
A substance that accepts electrons and causes oxidation of another substance

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the trend in one sentence without explanation, e.g. 'Electronegativity decreases down Group 17'
Give the trend plus reason using atomic structure, e.g. 'because atomic radius increases'
Give observations AND equations for reactions
Use the established trend to determine properties of unknown halogens

modelAnswer.question: Explain why chlorine displaces bromine from potassium bromide solution. Write an equation for this reaction and state what you would observe.
modelAnswer.answer: Chlorine is more reactive than bromine because chlorine atoms are smaller so can gain electrons more easily. Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂. The colourless solution turns orange/brown due to bromine formation.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Chlorine is more reactive than bromine
Mark 2: Explanation using atomic size/electron gain

commonMistakes: Never say 'iodine is purple' in solution - it is brown in aqueous solution, purple only in non-polar solvents
Students often write incorrect formulas for disproportionation - remember ClO⁻ is chlorate(I), not chlorate(V)
Don't confuse oxidising ability with reducing ability - they are opposite trends down the group
Never say 'halogens become more reactive down the group' - reactivity decreases down Group 17
Students forget that displacement reactions only work if the free halogen is MORE reactive than the halide
examinerTips: Halogen displacement reactions appear frequently - learn the colour changes for Cl₂/Br₂/I₂
Always state observations AND write equations for displacement reactions
Remember disproportionation only occurs with cold dilute alkali - hot concentrated gives different products
Use silver nitrate test for halide identification - learn the precipitate colours
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Test tube diagrams showing colour changes in displacement reactions: colourless KBr turning orange with Cl₂, colourless KI turning brown with Cl₂ or Br₂
